title: Hyderabadi Haleem (Home-Style)

subs.name: bone-in chicken (reduce pressure to 4 whistles) for a quicker version

ingredients.name: broken wheat / daliya (fine semolina works as a shortcut)
visual_checks: soak 30 minutes if using daliya; skip soak for rava
ingredients.notes: soak 30 minutes if using daliya; skip soak for rava

subs.name: fine broken wheat (daliya) soaked overnight — more traditional texture
masoor dal (red lentil) — cooks faster and blends in completely
good cooking oil for a lighter finish

steps.text: Deep-fry half the sliced onions in oil until golden-brown and crispy; set aside for garnish. In the remaining oil in the pressure cooker, sauté the rest of the onions until soft.
Add ginger-garlic paste to the softened onions and fry for 2 minutes until the raw smell leaves. Add mutton pieces and sear on high heat for 5 minutes, turning until well coloured on all sides.

Add red chilli powder, turmeric, coriander powder, garam masala, and salt. Stir for 1 minute. Add the soaked chana dal, moong dal, and the rava (or soaked broken wheat). Pour in 1 litre water and stir everything together.
Close the pressure cooker and cook on high until the first whistle, then lower to medium and cook for 8 more whistles. Switch off and allow the pressure to release naturally, about 15 minutes.

Open the cooker. The mutton should be fall-off-the-bone tender. Use two forks to shred the meat directly in the pot, discarding any large bones. The mixture should be very thick and porridge-like — if too thin, simmer uncovered for 10 minutes, stirring often to prevent sticking.

Taste and adjust salt. Stir in half the mint and coriander leaves. Ladle into bowls. Top each serving with a spoonful of ghee, a squeeze of lemon, the crispy fried onions, and fresh mint and coriander. Serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mutton → bone-in chicken (reduce pressure to 4 whistles) for a quicker version; rava → fine broken wheat (daliya) soaked overnight — more traditional texture; ghee → good cooking oil for a lighter finish; chana-dal → masoor dal (red lentil) — cooks faster and blends in completely
